regulations.name: General Data Protection Regulation
regulations.authority: European Data Protection Board (EDPB)
regulations.summary: The world's most comprehensive data protection law, governing how organizations collect, store, process, and transfer personal data of individuals in the EU. It applies to any organization worldwide that handles EU resident data, regardless of where the organization is based.
key_requirements: Establish a lawful basis for every processing activity
Respect data subject rights: access, erasure, portability, rectification, objection
Report personal data breaches to supervisory authority within 72 hours
Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) for high-risk processing activities
Implement Privacy by Design and Privacy by Default
Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk activities
Ensure valid consent is freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous
Restrict transfers of personal data outside the EEA without adequate safeguards
regulations.penalties: Up to 20 million EUR or 4% of global annual turnover (whichever is higher)

regulations.name: Network and Information Security Directive 2
regulations.authority: European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA)
regulations.summary: A landmark EU directive that significantly expands cybersecurity obligations for organizations operating critical infrastructure and essential services. NIS2 broadens the scope of its predecessor to include more sectors and introduces stricter requirements for risk management, incident reporting, and supply chain security.
key_requirements: Implement risk management measures including policies on risk analysis and information system security
Report significant cybersecurity incidents to national authorities within 24 hours (initial) and 72 hours (detailed)
Ensure business continuity and crisis management capabilities
Address supply chain security and manage third-party risks
Implement vulnerability handling and disclosure processes
Train staff and promote cybersecurity awareness
Ensure security of network and information systems including encryption
Senior management accountability for compliance
regulations.penalties: Up to 10 million EUR or 2% of global annual turnover for essential entities

regulations.name: Digital Operational Resilience Act
regulations.authority: European Supervisory Authorities (EBA, EIOPA, ESMA)
regulations.summary: A regulation that establishes uniform requirements for the digital operational resilience of financial entities in the EU. DORA consolidates and upgrades ICT risk requirements previously scattered across multiple sectoral regulations, specifically for banks, insurers, investment firms, and their critical ICT third-party providers.
key_requirements: Establish an ICT Risk Management Framework with governance and accountability
Conduct regular Digital Operational Resilience Testing including advanced threat-led penetration testing (TLPT)
Detect, manage, classify, and report major ICT-related incidents
Manage ICT third-party risk with robust contractual requirements and exit strategies
Share cybersecurity threat intelligence among financial entities
Maintain and test business continuity and disaster recovery plans
Critical ICT third-party providers (CTPPs) must register and comply with ENISA oversight
regulations.penalties: Up to 2% of global annual turnover; periodic penalty payments up to 1% of average daily turnover

regulations.name: EU Artificial Intelligence Act
regulations.authority: EU AI Office (European Commission)
regulations.summary: The EU Artificial Intelligence Act is the world's first comprehensive AI regulation, entered into force on 1 August 2024. It applies a risk-based approach: prohibited AI practices banned from 2 February 2025; rules for General-Purpose AI (GPAI) models apply from 2 August 2025; high-risk AI system requirements apply from 2 August 2026. The AI Act covers providers, deployers, and importers of AI systems in the EU/EEA regardless of where the provider is established.
key_requirements: Prohibit unacceptable-risk AI systems including real-time biometric surveillance in public spaces and social scoring
High-risk AI systems must undergo conformity assessments and register in EU database
Maintain technical documentation, logs, and human oversight for high-risk AI
Ensure transparency: users must know when interacting with AI (chatbots, deepfakes)
General-purpose AI model providers must disclose training data and conduct adversarial testing
Establish a quality management system for high-risk AI systems
Post-market monitoring and incident reporting for high-risk AI
regulations.penalties: Up to 35 million EUR or 7% of global annual turnover for prohibited AI violations

regulations.name: EU Cybersecurity Act
regulations.authority: European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA)
regulations.summary: A regulation that grants ENISA a permanent mandate and establishes an EU-wide cybersecurity certification framework for ICT products, services, and processes.
key_requirements: Voluntary European cybersecurity certification schemes for ICT products, services, and processes
Three assurance levels: basic, substantial, and high based on risk profile
Manufacturers can self-declare conformity at the basic level; independent assessment required for high level
National certification supervisory authorities must be designated by each member state
ENISA maintains a database of all issued, amended, and withdrawn certificates
Cybersecurity certification does not replace sector-specific requirements (e.g., NIS2, DORA)
regulations.penalties: Defined by national laws; administrative fines for misleading certification claims

regulations.name: California Consumer Privacy Act (as amended by CPRA)
regulations.authority: California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA)
regulations.summary: California's comprehensive privacy law giving residents broad rights over their personal information. Considered the most stringent U.S. state privacy law and a model for other states.
key_requirements: Disclose categories of personal information collected and purposes for processing
Honor consumer rights: access, deletion, correction, and opt-out of sale/sharing
Right to limit use of sensitive personal information (SPI)
Opt-out of automated decision-making, including profiling
Conduct and document risk assessments for high-risk processing activities
Honor opt-out preference signals (Global Privacy Control) automatically
No retaliation for exercising privacy rights
regulations.penalties: Up to $2,500 per unintentional violation; $7,500 per intentional violation

regulations.name: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
regulations.authority: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) - Office for Civil Rights (OCR)
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law establishing national standards for protecting the privacy and security of protected health information (PHI). Applies to covered entities and business associates.
key_requirements: Privacy Rule: Safeguard PHI and give patients rights to access and amend their health records
Security Rule: Implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards for electronic PHI (ePHI)
Breach Notification Rule: Notify affected individuals, HHS, and media within 60 days of a breach
Minimum Necessary standard: only access or disclose the minimum PHI needed
Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) required for all third-party PHI handlers
Conduct a comprehensive risk analysis and implement a risk management plan
Designate a Privacy Officer and a Security Officer
regulations.penalties: Tiered: $100-$50,000 per violation; maximum $1.9 million per violation category per year

regulations.name: Sarbanes-Oxley Act
regulations.authority: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB)
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law establishing enhanced standards for all U.S. publicly traded companies and their auditors, focused on financial reporting integrity and internal controls.
key_requirements: CEO and CFO must personally certify the accuracy of financial reports
Assess and document internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR) annually (Section 404)
External auditor must independently attest to management's ICFR assessment
Establish and maintain an audit committee with financial expert requirements
Maintain document retention policies; destruction of audit records is a criminal offense
Establish whistleblower protection programs and anonymous reporting mechanisms
regulations.penalties: Up to $5 million in fines and 20 years imprisonment for securities fraud

regulations.name: Executive Order 14028 - Improving the Nation's Cybersecurity
regulations.authority: U.S. Office of Management and Budget (OMB), CISA, NIST
regulations.summary: U.S. presidential executive order mandating sweeping cybersecurity improvements across the federal government, catalyzing Zero Trust architecture, SBOM requirements, and software supply chain security.
key_requirements: Federal agencies must adopt Zero Trust Architecture (ZTA) following NIST SP 800-207
Software vendors selling to the federal government must provide a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)
Implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) and encryption for federal systems within 180 days
Establish a Cyber Safety Review Board (CSRB) to review significant cyber incidents
Mandate use of endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions across federal agencies
Standardize cybersecurity incident playbooks across federal agencies
regulations.penalties: N/A (executive order - non-compliance risks contract termination for federal vendors)

regulations.name: Personal Information Protection Law
regulations.authority: Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC)
regulations.summary: China's first comprehensive personal information protection law, drawing inspiration from the GDPR while incorporating distinctive Chinese characteristics. Imposes strict cross-border data transfer restrictions and data localization requirements.
key_requirements: Obtain separate consent for each processing purpose; bundled consents are invalid
Conduct Personal Information Protection Impact Assessments (PIPIAs) before high-risk processing
Designate a Personal Information Protection Officer (PIPO)
Strict cross-border data transfer restrictions: standard contracts, security assessments, or certification required
Critical information infrastructure operators and large-scale processors must store data in China
Notify authorities and individuals within specified timeframes of breaches
regulations.penalties: Up to RMB 50 million (approx. USD 7 million) or 5% of prior year's annual turnover

regulations.name: Lei Geral de Protecao de Dados (Brazilian General Data Protection Law)
regulations.authority: Autoridade Nacional de Protecao de Dados (ANPD)
regulations.summary: Brazil's comprehensive data protection law, heavily influenced by the GDPR. Applies to any processing of personal data performed in Brazil or directed at individuals in Brazil.
key_requirements: Establish a lawful basis for processing (10 legal bases)
Respect data subject rights including access, correction, deletion, portability
Report data security incidents that may cause risk or harm to the ANPD
Appoint a Data Protection Officer (Encarregado)
Conduct Data Protection Impact Reports for high-risk processing
International data transfers require adequate protection or specific safeguards
regulations.penalties: Up to R$50 million (~USD 10 million) per violation or 2% of Brazilian revenue

regulations.name: Personal Data Protection Law
regulations.authority: Saudi Data and AI Authority (SDAIA) / National Data Management Office (NDMO)
regulations.summary: Saudi Arabia's first comprehensive data protection law establishing rights for individuals and obligations for organizations processing personal data of individuals in Saudi Arabia.
key_requirements: Obtain explicit consent for processing personal data or rely on alternative legal bases
Notify data subjects of processing purposes and their rights
Respect individual rights: access, correction, erasure, and objection
Report data breaches to SDAIA and affected individuals within 72 hours
Conduct Data Transfer Impact Assessments before cross-border transfers
Store data relating to Saudi citizens or residents within Saudi Arabia (data localization)
Appoint a Data Protection Officer for high-risk processing activities
regulations.penalties: Up to SAR 5 million (~USD 1.3 million) for violations; criminal penalties for intentional violations

regulations.name: Protection of Privacy Law and Privacy Protection Regulations (Data Security)
regulations.authority: Privacy Protection Authority (PPA)
regulations.summary: Israel's foundational data protection framework combining the Protection of Privacy Law (1981) with the comprehensive 2017 Data Security Regulations, adding prescriptive security requirements including database classification, security officer requirements, risk assessments, and breach notification obligations.
key_requirements: Register databases containing personal information with the Registrar of Databases (PPA)
Classify data sensitivity level and implement proportionate security controls
Appoint a Database Security Officer for medium and high-security databases
Conduct annual internal audits and risk assessments
Report severe security incidents to PPA and affected individuals within 72 hours
Implement access controls, logging, and user authorization procedures
Data subject rights: access, correction, and deletion of personal information
Restrict transfer of data to countries without adequate protection
regulations.penalties: Fines up to NIS 70,000 per violation; criminal liability for willful violations; civil damages

regulations.name: Israel National Cyber Directorate Cyber Defense Directive
regulations.authority: Israel National Cyber Directorate (INCD)
regulations.summary: Framework issued by the Israel National Cyber Directorate establishing binding cybersecurity requirements for critical infrastructure organizations across all sectors including energy, water, finance, health, transportation, and communications.
key_requirements: Annual cyber risk assessment and security program update
Appoint a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) with defined authorities
Implement network segmentation between operational technology (OT) and IT systems
Report significant cybersecurity incidents to INCD within 24 hours
Maintain and test business continuity and disaster recovery plans
Employee cybersecurity awareness training program
Third-party and supply chain risk management requirements
Vulnerability management and patching program with defined timelines
regulations.penalties: Administrative sanctions from sector regulators; critical infrastructure operators may face license restrictions

regulations.name: Prohibition on Money Laundering Law
regulations.authority: Israel Money Laundering and Terror Financing Prohibition Authority (IMPA)
regulations.summary: Israeli law prohibiting money laundering and the financing of terrorism, requiring financial institutions, money service businesses, insurance companies, attorneys, and accountants to implement AML/CFT programs. Israel is a FATF member and aligns its regime with FATF recommendations.
key_requirements: Customer due diligence (CDD/KYC) for all customers prior to establishing a business relationship
Enhanced due diligence for high-risk customers, politically exposed persons (PEPs), and complex transactions
File suspicious transaction reports (STRs) with IMPA without tipping off the customer
Maintain records of all transactions and customer identification for at least 7 years
Appoint an AML compliance officer and implement an internal AML program
Staff training on recognizing and reporting suspicious activity
Comply with FATF Recommendations and UN Security Council resolutions on terrorism financing
regulations.penalties: Up to 10 years imprisonment for money laundering; fines up to 3x the laundered amount; regulatory sanctions for entities

regulations.name: Protection of Personal Information Act
regulations.authority: Information Regulator (South Africa)
regulations.summary: South Africa's comprehensive data protection law establishing eight lawful conditions for processing personal information. Closely modeled on the EU GDPR, POPIA applies to all public and private bodies that process personal information and creates the independent Information Regulator as enforcement authority.
key_requirements: Accountability: designate and register an Information Officer with the Information Regulator
Processing limitation: collect only for lawful, specific, explicitly defined, and legitimate purposes
Purpose specification: retain information only as long as necessary
Further processing limitation: must be compatible with original purpose
Information quality: take steps to ensure personal information is complete, accurate, and up to date
Security safeguards: implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures
Data subject participation: rights to access, correction, and deletion of personal information
Notify the Information Regulator and affected data subjects of security compromises
regulations.penalties: Up to ZAR 10 million fine or 10 years imprisonment for certain offenses; civil damages available

regulations.name: Financial Intelligence Centre Act
regulations.authority: Financial Intelligence Centre (FIC)
regulations.summary: South Africa's primary AML/CFT legislation requiring accountable institutions (banks, insurers, estate agents, legal practitioners, accountants, casinos) to implement customer due diligence and suspicious transaction reporting. The 2017 amendments introduced a risk-based approach to combat money laundering, terrorist financing, and proliferation financing.
key_requirements: Risk-based customer due diligence (CDD) including identification and verification of beneficial owners
Enhanced due diligence for high-risk customers, complex transactions, and PEPs
File suspicious and unusual transaction reports (SUTRs) to the FIC
File cash threshold reports (CTRs) for cash transactions exceeding ZAR 49,999
Keep records of client identification and transactions for at least 5 years
Appoint a Compliance Officer and maintain an AML/CFT risk management and compliance program
Ongoing staff training on AML/CFT obligations
regulations.penalties: Up to ZAR 100 million fine or 15 years imprisonment; administrative sanctions by FIC

regulations.name: Kenya Data Protection Act
regulations.authority: Office of the Data Protection Commissioner (ODPC)
regulations.summary: Kenya's first comprehensive data protection law establishing principles for the processing of personal data, rights of data subjects, and obligations of data controllers and processors. Applies to any person who processes personal data about Kenyan residents.
key_requirements: Register as a data controller or processor with the ODPC if processing above prescribed thresholds
Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO)
Provide privacy notices disclosing processing purposes and data subject rights
Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, portability, restriction, and objection
Obtain consent or establish lawful basis for processing personal data
Notify the ODPC and data subjects of data breaches within 72 hours
Conduct privacy impact assessments for high-risk processing
Restrict cross-border transfers unless adequate protection is ensured
regulations.penalties: Up to KES 3 million (~USD 23,000) or 10 years imprisonment per violation

regulations.name: Nigeria Data Protection Act
regulations.authority: Nigeria Data Protection Commission (NDPC)
regulations.summary: Nigeria's comprehensive data protection law replacing the Nigeria Data Protection Regulation (NDPR) 2019. Creates the Nigeria Data Protection Commission with independent status and introduces significant penalties for non-compliance.
key_requirements: Establish a lawful basis for processing personal data
Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, portability, restriction, and objection
Notify the NDPC and affected data subjects of data breaches within 72 hours
Data protection compliance audit annually for high-volume data processors
Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO)
Cross-border data transfer safeguards or NDPC authorization required
Privacy notices in plain language at point of collection
Children's data requires parental or guardian consent
regulations.penalties: Up to 2% of annual gross revenue in Nigeria or NGN 10 million (~USD 6,500) whichever is higher

regulations.name: Ghana Data Protection Act
regulations.authority: Data Protection Commission (DPC) of Ghana
regulations.summary: One of Africa's earliest comprehensive data protection laws, requiring organizations to register with the Data Protection Commission and comply with data protection principles. Establishes individual rights over personal data and imposes obligations on data controllers.
key_requirements: Register data processing systems with the Data Protection Commission
Appoint a data controller responsible for compliance
Comply with data protection principles: collection limitation, data quality, purpose specification, use limitation, security safeguards, openness
Data subject rights: access to personal information and correction of inaccuracies
Obtain consent of data subjects for processing personal data
Implement security measures to protect against unauthorized access and data breaches
Cross-border data transfers only to countries with adequate protection
regulations.penalties: Fines and possible imprisonment for serious breaches; DPC can issue enforcement notices

regulations.name: Rwanda Law on Protection of Personal Data and Privacy
regulations.authority: Rwanda National Cyber Security Authority (NCSA)
regulations.summary: Rwanda's comprehensive law on the protection of personal data and privacy, aligning with international data protection standards. Establishes principles for data processing, individual rights, and obligations for data controllers and processors operating in Rwanda.
key_requirements: Process personal data only for specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes
Obtain consent of data subjects or rely on another lawful basis
Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, restriction, and objection
Implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures
Notify data subjects of data breaches that may cause significant harm
Conduct Privacy Impact Assessments for high-risk processing activities
Appoint a Data Protection Officer for large-scale processing
Cross-border data transfers require adequate protection safeguards
regulations.penalties: Fines and criminal penalties per Rwanda Penal Code; NCSA can issue compliance orders

regulations.name: Egypt Personal Data Protection Law
regulations.authority: Personal Data Protection Centre (PDPC) - Ministry of Communications and IT
regulations.summary: Egypt's first comprehensive personal data protection law establishing a legal framework for the collection, storage, processing, and transfer of personal data. Requires explicit consent as the primary basis for processing and introduces a licensing regime for data controllers.
key_requirements: Obtain explicit consent for processing personal data (consent is primary legal basis)
Provide privacy notices in Arabic covering processing purposes and data subject rights
Data subject rights: access, rectification, erasure, and data portability
Notify the PDPC of data breaches in a timely manner
Restrictions on cross-border data transfers - requires prior PDPC authorization
Obtain license from PDPC before processing sensitive or biometric data
Appoint a Data Protection Officer for processors licensed by the PDPC
Implement technical and organizational security measures proportionate to risk
regulations.penalties: Fines from EGP 50,000 to EGP 2 million (~USD 1,600-65,000); criminal penalties up to 3 years imprisonment

regulations.name: Morocco Law on Protection of Personal Data (Law 09-08)
regulations.authority: Commission Nationale de controle de la protection des Donnees a caractere Personnel (CNDP)
regulations.summary: Morocco's foundational data protection law establishing principles for processing personal data, individual rights, and obligations for data controllers. Requires organizations to declare or obtain authorization for data processing activities from the CNDP.
key_requirements: Declare or obtain prior authorization from CNDP for data processing activities
Lawful basis for processing required - consent is primary
Data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, and objection to processing
Cross-border data transfers restricted to countries with adequate protection
Special category data (health, biometric, religion, political opinion) requires CNDP prior authorization
Security measures to protect personal data from unauthorized access or disclosure
Retention limitation: data must not be kept longer than necessary
regulations.penalties: Fines up to MAD 300,000 (~USD 30,000) and imprisonment 3-12 months; CNDP can issue compliance orders

regulations.name: UAE Federal Personal Data Protection Law
regulations.authority: UAE Data Office
regulations.summary: The UAE's first federal personal data protection law (Federal Decree-Law No. 45 of 2021) applying to all entities processing personal data of UAE residents outside of free zones. Establishes data protection principles, individual rights, and creates the UAE Data Office.
key_requirements: Lawful basis for processing: consent, contractual necessity, legitimate interests, or legal obligation
Data subject rights: access, correction, erasure, restriction, and objection
Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) if processing personal data on a large scale
Notify the UAE Data Office of personal data breaches within 72 hours
Cross-border data transfers only to approved countries or with appropriate safeguards
Data minimization and purpose limitation principles apply to all processing
Implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures
regulations.penalties: Fines up to AED 5 million (~USD 1.4 million) for violations; serious violations up to AED 20 million

regulations.name: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act
regulations.authority: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC)
regulations.summary: Canada's federal private-sector privacy law governing the collection, use, and disclosure of personal information in commercial activities. Based on 10 Fair Information Principles from the CSA Model Code. Mandatory breach reporting was added in 2018.
key_requirements: 10 Fair Information Principles: accountability, identifying purposes, consent, limiting collection, limiting use/disclosure/retention, accuracy, safeguards, openness, individual access, challenging compliance
Obtain meaningful consent before collecting, using, or disclosing personal information
Mandatory breach reporting to OPC for breaches posing real risk of significant harm
Notify affected individuals of breaches posing real risk of significant harm
Maintain breach records for at least 24 months
Appoint a Privacy Officer and make their contact information available
Honor individual access requests within 30 days
regulations.penalties: Up to CAD 100,000 for willful contraventions; enforcement via OPC investigation and Federal Court orders

regulations.name: Quebec Act to Modernize Privacy Laws (Law 25)
regulations.authority: Commission d'acces a l'information du Quebec (CAI)
regulations.summary: Quebec's sweeping modernization of its privacy law, often described as the strictest privacy law in Canada and closely aligned with the GDPR. Implemented in three phases (2022-2024), introduces mandatory PIAs, right to erasure, data portability, and GDPR-level penalties.
key_requirements: Mandatory Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs) before implementing technology involving personal information
Right to erasure (be forgotten) for individuals
Right to data portability in a commonly used technological format
Disclosure of automated decision-making when exclusively based on personal information
Explicit consent in clear and simple language; bundled consent not permitted
Appoint a Privacy Officer and publish their contact information on the organization's website
Notify the CAI and individuals of confidentiality incidents within 72 hours
Cross-border transfer safeguards assessed through Privacy Impact Assessments
regulations.penalties: Up to CAD 25 million or 4% of worldwide turnover - equivalent to GDPR penalties

regulations.name: Canada's Anti-Spam Legislation
regulations.authority: Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission (CRTC), Competition Bureau, OPC
regulations.summary: One of the world's strictest anti-spam laws, requiring express or implied consent before sending commercial electronic messages (CEMs), installing computer programs, or collecting email addresses through electronic means.
key_requirements: Express or implied consent required before sending commercial electronic messages (CEMs)
Identify the sender clearly in every message including name and contact information
Provide a functioning unsubscribe mechanism in every CEM
Honor unsubscribe requests within 10 business days
Express consent required before installing computer programs on third-party devices
Maintain records proving consent for at least 3 years
Implied consent expires after 2 years (for most business relationships)
regulations.penalties: Up to CAD 10 million per violation for organizations; CAD 1 million for individuals

regulations.name: OSFI Guideline B-13: Technology and Cyber Risk Management
regulations.authority: Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI)
regulations.summary: OSFI's binding guideline setting out technology and cyber risk management expectations for all federally regulated financial institutions (FRFIs) in Canada including banks, insurance companies, and trust companies.
key_requirements: Board-level cyber risk oversight, accountability, and risk appetite definition
Technology and cyber risk identification, assessment, and management program
Cyber resilience testing including penetration testing and cyber war-gaming exercises
Third-party and supply chain technology risk management requirements
Notify OSFI of technology or cyber incidents within 72 hours
Scenario-based stress testing for technology and cyber incidents
Data security classification and encryption standards implementation
Annual board report on technology and cyber risk
regulations.penalties: OSFI may issue compliance orders, increase capital requirements, or restrict activities for non-compliant FRFIs

regulations.name: Argentina Personal Data Protection Act (Ley 25.326)
regulations.authority: Agencia de Acceso a la Informacion Publica (AAIP)
regulations.summary: Argentina's foundational data protection law and one of the first in Latin America to receive EU adequacy recognition (2003). Establishes principles for processing personal data, rights of data subjects, and requires registration of databases.
key_requirements: Register personal information databases with the AAIP's Registro Nacional de Bases de Datos
Obtain informed consent for the processing of personal data
Data subject rights: access, rectification, suppression, and confidentiality
Special protection for sensitive data (health, political opinions, religion, race, sexual orientation)
Cross-border transfers only to countries with adequate protection levels or with AAIP authorization
Security measures proportionate to the nature and amount of data stored
Prohibition on selling databases to unauthorized third parties
regulations.penalties: Fines up to ARS 3 million per violation (law under modernization with much higher proposed fines)

regulations.name: Colombia Statutory Law 1581 on Personal Data Protection
regulations.authority: Superintendencia de Industria y Comercio (SIC)
regulations.summary: Colombia's comprehensive data protection law guaranteeing the fundamental constitutional right to habeas data. Requires prior express authorization (consent) as the primary lawful basis. The SIC supervises compliance.
key_requirements: Obtain prior express authorization (consent) from data subjects before processing personal data
Register databases with the National Registry of Databases (RNBD) administered by SIC
Privacy notices informing data subjects of their rights and processing purposes
Data subject rights: access, update, rectification, deletion, and revocation of authorization
Appoint a Data Protection Officer (internal or external)
Sign data processor agreements with all third-party processors
Sensitive data (race, political opinion, health, sexual orientation) requires explicit consent
Cross-border transfers require guarantees of adequate protection
regulations.penalties: Fines up to COP 2,000 million (~USD 500,000); suspension of data processing activities

regulations.name: Chile Personal Data Protection Law (Ley 19.628 reformed by Ley 21.719)
regulations.authority: Agencia de Proteccion de Datos Personales (APDP - established under Ley 21.719)
regulations.summary: Chile's personal data protection law. A landmark reform (Ley 21.719, enacted December 2024) takes full effect in 2026-2027, introducing GDPR-level rights and creating a dedicated Agencia de Proteccion de Datos.
key_requirements: Lawful basis for processing - consent is primary lawful basis under current law
Data subject rights: access, correction, deletion, blocking, and transfer
Data accuracy principle: data must be exact, complete, and current
Security obligations to protect personal data from unauthorized access
Sensitive data categories require express consent
Under Ley 21.719 (from 2026): GDPR-like rights including portability; mandatory DPO; PIAs; 72-hour breach notification
regulations.penalties: Current law: civil damages only; Ley 21.719 (from 2026): up to 2% of annual revenue for serious violations

regulations.name: Mexico Federal Law on Protection of Personal Data Held by Private Parties
regulations.authority: Instituto Nacional de Transparencia, Acceso a la Informacion y Proteccion de Datos (INAI)
regulations.summary: Mexico's federal data protection law for the private sector. Mexico is one of only three non-EU countries with limited EU adequacy recognition. Requires organizations to publish privacy notices (avisos de privacidad) and respond to ARCO rights requests.
key_requirements: Publish a Privacy Notice (aviso de privacidad) at or before the point of data collection
Obtain consent for data processing; explicit consent for sensitive data
Honor ARCO rights - Access, Rectification, Cancellation, Opposition - within 20 business days
Sensitive data (health, biometric, racial origin, beliefs) requires explicit consent
Implement appropriate security measures based on data volume and sensitivity
Notify INAI and affected individuals of data security breaches
Sign data processing agreements with third-party processors
Designate a data protection department or person responsible for compliance
regulations.penalties: Fines up to MXN 320 million (~USD 16 million); criminal sanctions for egregious violations

regulations.name: Ecuador Organic Law on Personal Data Protection
regulations.authority: Superintendencia de Proteccion de Datos Personales
regulations.summary: Ecuador's comprehensive data protection law closely modeled on the GDPR, establishing rights for data subjects and obligations for controllers and processors. Applies to any organization processing personal data of individuals located in Ecuador.
key_requirements: Lawful basis for processing required - consent, contractual necessity, legitimate interests, legal obligation
Data subject rights: access, rectification, deletion, portability, objection, restriction, and right not to be subject to automated decisions
Notify the Superintendencia of data breaches within 72 hours
Appoint a Data Protection Delegate (DPO equivalent) for large-scale processing
Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk processing
Cross-border data transfer restrictions with adequate protection requirement
Privacy by design and privacy by default principles
regulations.penalties: Up to 1% of annual gross turnover for minor violations; up to 2% for serious violations

regulations.name: Uruguay Law on Personal Data Protection and Habeas Data Action
regulations.authority: Unidad Reguladora y de Control de Datos Personales (URCDP)
regulations.summary: Uruguay's data protection law, the first in Latin America to receive EU adequacy recognition (2012). Establishes principles for data processing, individual rights including a constitutional habeas data action, and requires database registration.
key_requirements: Register databases containing personal data with the URCDP
Data subject rights: access, rectification, suppression, and the constitutional habeas data action
Informed consent for collecting and processing personal data
Sensitive data protection requirements
International data transfers only to countries with adequate protection or URCDP authorization
Appoint a representative contact for data subjects
Security measures appropriate to the nature and volume of data stored
regulations.penalties: Administrative sanctions by URCDP; judicial remedies through habeas data action; civil damages

regulations.name: Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act - FTC Safeguards Rule
regulations.authority: Federal Trade Commission (FTC), federal banking regulators (OCC, FDIC, Federal Reserve)
regulations.summary: Federal law requiring financial institutions to protect the privacy and security of customers' non-public personal financial information. The FTC's updated 2023 Safeguards Rule strengthened security requirements for non-banking financial institutions including auto dealers, mortgage brokers, payday lenders, tax preparers, and financial advisors.
key_requirements: Designate a qualified individual to oversee the information security program
Conduct annual risk assessments identifying reasonably foreseeable threats
Implement access controls, multi-factor authentication, and encryption for customer information
Develop and test an incident response plan
Notify the FTC within 30 days of a data breach affecting 500+ customers
Oversee third-party service providers with security requirements in contracts
Board-level annual report on the status of the information security program
Monitor and test effectiveness of security controls annually
regulations.penalties: FTC enforcement; civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation for institutions; $10,000 per violation for officers and directors

regulations.name: Children's Online Privacy Protection Act
regulations.authority: Federal Trade Commission (FTC)
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law placing parents in control over the online collection of personal information from children under 13. Applies to operators of commercial websites, online services, and apps directed at children or with actual knowledge they are collecting information from children under 13.
key_requirements: Post a clear and comprehensive privacy policy describing information collection practices
Obtain verifiable parental consent before collecting personal information from children under 13
Allow parents to review, correct, and delete their child's personal information
Not condition a child's participation on providing more information than necessary
Maintain reasonable security procedures to protect children's personal information
Retain personal information only as long as reasonably necessary
Verify age before collecting data - COPPA 2.0 expands protections to under 16
regulations.penalties: Civil penalties up to $51,744 per violation per day (adjusted for inflation); multi-million dollar FTC settlements common

regulations.name: Federal Information Security Modernization Act
regulations.authority: CISA, Office of Management and Budget (OMB), NIST, Agency Inspector Generals
regulations.summary: U.S. federal law requiring all federal agencies and their contractors to develop, document, and implement information security programs protecting government information and systems. Mandates use of the NIST Risk Management Framework.
key_requirements: Develop an agency-wide information security program using the NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF)
Categorize systems as low, moderate, or high impact per FIPS 199
Select and implement security controls from NIST SP 800-53
Conduct independent security assessments and obtain Authorization to Operate (ATO)
Continuously monitor all security controls via Continuous Diagnostics and Mitigation (CDM)
Report cybersecurity metrics annually to OMB and DHS
Contractor systems holding federal data must comply with FISMA requirements
Inspector General conducts annual independent evaluation of agency security programs
regulations.penalties: Non-criminal; non-compliance may result in reduced agency funding, Inspector General findings, and binding operational directives from CISA

regulations.name: SEC Cybersecurity Risk Management and Incident Disclosure Rules
regulations.authority: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC)
regulations.summary: SEC rules requiring public companies to disclose material cybersecurity incidents within 4 business days on Form 8-K, and to provide annual disclosures (Form 10-K) about cybersecurity risk management, strategy, and board governance.
key_requirements: Report material cybersecurity incidents on Form 8-K within 4 business days of materiality determination
Annual 10-K disclosures of cybersecurity risk management processes and strategy
Describe board oversight of cybersecurity risks and management's cybersecurity expertise and role
Disclose material effects of past cybersecurity incidents on financial condition and operations
Foreign private issuers must use Form 20-F/6-K equivalents
Define materiality using the same standard as other securities disclosures
regulations.penalties: SEC enforcement actions; material misstatements in public filings subject to securities fraud liability; penalties up to $5 million per violation

regulations.name: Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification 2.0
regulations.authority: U.S. Department of Defense (DoD)
regulations.summary: DoD's cybersecurity certification program requiring defense contractors and subcontractors handling Federal Contract Information (FCI) or Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) to achieve and maintain certified cybersecurity postures.
key_requirements: Level 1 (Foundational): 17 basic practices per FAR 52.204-21; annual self-assessment and senior official affirmation
Level 2 (Advanced): 110 practices per NIST SP 800-171; triennial C3PAO third-party assessment for critical programs
Level 3 (Expert): 24 additional practices per NIST SP 800-172; government-led assessment by DCSA
Plans of Action and Milestones (POA&Ms) allowed for Level 1 and 2 assessment gaps
Affirmation by a senior official confirming compliance annually
Flow-down requirements: prime contractors must impose CMMC on subcontractors handling FCI/CUI
regulations.penalties: False claims on contractor attestations may trigger False Claims Act liability (treble damages); contract disqualification for non-compliant vendors

regulations.name: New York DFS Cybersecurity Regulation (23 NYCRR Part 500) — 2023 Amendment
regulations.authority: New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS)
regulations.summary: The New York Department of Financial Services cybersecurity regulation (23 NYCRR Part 500) imposes prescriptive security requirements on all DFS-licensed entities. Originally effective March 2017, a major amendment effective November 1, 2023 substantially expanded requirements: mandatory multi-factor authentication for all systems, 24-hour incident notification to NYDFS, enhanced CISO requirements, annual penetration testing, vulnerability management, and board-level cybersecurity accountability.
key_requirements: Implement a cybersecurity program based on a formal risk assessment
Appoint a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) responsible for cybersecurity program
Annual penetration testing and bi-annual vulnerability assessments
Multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all remote access, privileged accounts, and email systems
Notify NYDFS of a cybersecurity event within 72 hours if it has a reasonable likelihood of materially harming the business
Annual certification of compliance by a senior officer or the board
Third-party service provider security policy with contractual security requirements
Large covered entities must have independent audits and board cybersecurity expertise
regulations.penalties: Up to $250,000 per violation; up to $2,500 per day for extended violations; up to $1 million for serious or systemic violations (2023 amendments)

regulations.name: Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act
regulations.authority: Private right of action - enforced by courts (no state agency)
regulations.summary: The nation's most powerful biometric privacy law, prohibiting private entities from collecting, storing, or profiting from biometric identifiers (fingerprints, retinal scans, facial geometry, voiceprints, handprints) without prior informed written consent.
key_requirements: Obtain written informed consent before collecting any biometric identifier or biometric information
Publish a publicly available written retention and destruction policy
Destroy biometric data when the original purpose expires or within 3 years of collection
No sale or other profit from biometric data under any circumstances
No disclosure of biometric data without consent or court order
Provide individuals with the right to know what biometric data is being collected and why
regulations.penalties: $1,000 per negligent violation or $5,000 per intentional/reckless violation per person; no actual harm required; class actions regularly produce settlements exceeding $100 million

regulations.name: Virginia Consumer Data Protection Act
regulations.authority: Virginia Attorney General
regulations.summary: Virginia's comprehensive privacy law applying to controllers processing personal data of 100,000+ Virginia consumers annually. Modeled after the GDPR, establishes consumer rights and controller obligations with AG-only enforcement.
key_requirements: Data minimization - limit collection to what is adequate, relevant, and reasonably necessary
Purpose limitation - process data only for disclosed and compatible purposes
Consumer rights: access, correction, deletion, portability, opt-out of targeted advertising/sale/profiling
Data protection assessments for high-risk processing activities
Controller-processor agreements required
Respond to consumer requests within 45 days (with 45-day extension if needed)
Opt-in consent required for processing sensitive data categories
Recognize universal opt-out mechanisms (GPC) from 2024
regulations.penalties: Up to $7,500 per violation; Attorney General enforcement only (no private right of action)

regulations.name: Colorado Privacy Act
regulations.authority: Colorado Attorney General and District Attorneys
regulations.summary: Colorado's comprehensive privacy law requiring organizations to recognize universal opt-out mechanisms like Global Privacy Control (GPC). Applies to organizations controlling or processing personal data of 100,000+ Colorado consumers annually.
key_requirements: Consumer rights: access, correction, deletion, portability, opt-out of targeted advertising/sale/profiling
Mandatory recognition of universal opt-out mechanisms (GPC) since July 2024
Process opt-out signals from authorized agents without verification of the agent
Data protection assessments required for high-risk processing activities
Heightened consent requirements for sensitive data categories
Controller-processor data processing agreements required
Respond to consumer requests within 45 days (with 45-day extension available)
regulations.penalties: Up to $20,000 per violation; AG and District Attorney enforcement (no private right of action)

regulations.name: Australian Privacy Act 1988 with Australian Privacy Principles and NDB Scheme
regulations.authority: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC)
regulations.summary: Australia's primary privacy law applying to Australian Government agencies and most private-sector organizations with annual turnover over AUD 3 million. The 13 Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) govern all aspects of personal information handling. The Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) Scheme requires notification of eligible breaches.
key_requirements: 13 Australian Privacy Principles (APPs): open and transparent management, anonymity, collection limitations, use/disclosure limitations, direct marketing opt-out, cross-border disclosure safeguards, data quality, security safeguards, access and correction rights
Notify the OAIC and affected individuals of eligible data breaches within 30 days of becoming aware
Eligible breach: likely to result in serious harm to individuals whose information is involved
Obtain meaningful consent for sensitive information (health, genetic, racial, political, religious, sexual orientation)
Privacy Impact Assessments recommended for high-privacy-risk activities
Cross-border data disclosures: must ensure overseas recipient provides substantially similar protections
2024 amendments: new right to erasure, enhanced OAIC powers, statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy
regulations.penalties: Up to AUD 50 million per violation for serious or repeated breaches; individual penalties also apply under 2024 amendments

regulations.name: Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018 (SOCI)
regulations.authority: Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) / Cyber and Infrastructure Security Centre (CISC)
regulations.summary: Australian law imposing security obligations on owners and operators of critical infrastructure across 11 sectors: electricity, gas, water and sewerage, ports, data storage and processing, financial market infrastructure, food and grocery, healthcare and medical, space technology, broadcasting, and transport.
key_requirements: Register critical infrastructure assets with the Register of Critical Infrastructure Assets
Develop and maintain a Critical Infrastructure Risk Management Program (CIRMP) reviewed annually by the board
Report cybersecurity incidents to ASD within 12 hours for critical infrastructure systems
Report other serious security incidents within 72 hours
Disclose offshore ownership interests and notify of changes to ownership above thresholds
Enhanced cybersecurity obligations for designated Systems of National Significance
Government has step-in rights to direct response to major national cyber incidents
Notify ASD of system vulnerabilities that could result in significant harm
regulations.penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 250,000 per contravention; criminal penalties for most serious breaches

regulations.name: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006
regulations.authority: Australian Transaction Reports and Analysis Centre (AUSTRAC)
regulations.summary: Australia's AML/CTF regime applying to reporting entities providing designated services including banks, credit unions, money remitters, casinos, bullion dealers, and digital currency exchanges. Major 2024 reforms extended obligations to Tranche 2 entities including lawyers, accountants, and real estate agents.
key_requirements: Enroll or register with AUSTRAC as a reporting entity
Develop and maintain an AML/CTF program including risk assessment and customer due diligence procedures
Know Your Customer (KYC) / Customer Identification Program (CIP) for all customers
Transaction monitoring program to identify suspicious activity
File Suspicious Matter Reports (SMRs) to AUSTRAC within 24 hours of suspicion
File Threshold Transaction Reports (TTRs) for cash transactions of AUD 10,000+
File International Funds Transfer Instructions (IFTIs) for all international transfers
Tranche 2 entities (lawyers, accountants, real estate) must enroll from 2026
regulations.penalties: Civil penalties up to AUD 22 million per contravention; AUSTRAC fined Commonwealth Bank AUD 700M (2018), Westpac AUD 1.3B (2020)

regulations.name: Cyber Security Act 2024
regulations.authority: Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) / Department of Home Affairs
regulations.summary: Australia's first dedicated cybersecurity act, part of the 2023-2030 Australian Cyber Security Strategy. Establishes mandatory ransomware payment reporting, minimum security standards for smart connected devices (IoT), and a new Cyber Incident Review Board.
key_requirements: Mandatory reporting of ransomware payments to ASD within 72 hours (immunity from regulatory use of the report)
Minimum security standards for smart connected devices (IoT) sold in Australia - aligned with ETSI EN 303 645
Suppliers of smart devices must publish statements of compliance and support periods
Cyber Incident Review Board reviews significant cyber incidents to identify systemic improvements
Limited-use obligations: government assistance data can only be used to manage the incident, not for prosecution
Cyber security obligations for critical infrastructure interconnected with SOCI Act requirements
regulations.penalties: Civil penalties for non-reporting of ransomware payments; product liability for non-compliant smart devices

regulations.name: International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO)
regulations.summary: The most important international treaty on maritime safety, prescribing minimum standards for the construction, equipment, and operation of merchant ships. Covers fire safety, life-saving appliances, radio communications, navigational safety, carriage of cargoes and dangerous goods, ISM Code, and ISPS Code.
key_requirements: Construction standards: watertight integrity, stability, subdivision, and fire safety systems
Life-saving appliances: sufficient lifeboats, liferafts, EPIRBs, immersion suits, and fire extinguishers
Radio communications: GMDSS (Global Maritime Distress and Safety System) equipment installation
Navigation: mandatory ECDIS, AIS, voyage data recorder (VDR), and ARPA radar on applicable vessels
Chapter IX - ISM Code: mandatory Safety Management System for company and ships
Chapter XI-2 - ISPS Code: Ship Security Plan and continuous Synopsis Record
Five-year SOLAS certificates renewed via annual, intermediate, and renewal surveys
Port State Control: non-compliant ships may be detained until deficiencies are corrected
regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention of vessels; flag state may suspend, withdraw, or refuse to issue certificates; criminal liability under national law

regulations.name: International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL)
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO)
regulations.summary: The primary international convention preventing pollution from ships, covering six annexes (oil, noxious liquids, harmful packaged substances, sewage, garbage, air pollution). Key requirements include the 2020 global 0.5% sulfur cap on marine fuels and Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) ratings from 2023.
key_requirements: Annex I: maintain Oil Record Book; prohibit oil discharges except compliant bilge water via OCM
Annex II: chemical tankers must comply with IBC Code; pre-washing procedures before port discharge
Annex V: Garbage Management Plan; prohibited from discharging plastics and food waste in special areas
Annex VI: global sulfur cap 0.5% (0.1% in Emission Control Areas - North Sea, Baltic, North America, US Caribbean)
Annex VI: NOx Tier III standards for new ships in ECAs
Ship Energy Efficiency Management Plan (SEEMP) and Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) rating A-E from 2023
EEDI/EEXI requirements for new and existing ships to improve energy efficiency
IMO 2030/2050 GHG Strategy: 50% total GHG reduction by 2050 vs 2008
regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state criminal/civil penalties; national law penalties (U.S. Act to Prevent Pollution from Ships allows fines up to $25,000 per day)

regulations.name: International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO) - implemented via SOLAS Chapter XI-2
regulations.summary: International code for the security of ships and port facilities adopted after 9/11, applying to international cargo ships of 500 GT or more, all passenger ships, and port facilities serving international ships. IMO MSC-FAL.1/Circ.3 (2017) integrates cybersecurity into ISPS requirements.
key_requirements: Ship Security Assessment (SSA) identifying vulnerabilities and security risks
Implement a Ship Security Plan (SSP) addressing three security levels
Designate a Ship Security Officer (SSO) and a Company Security Officer (CSO)
Install a Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) transmitting covert distress alert
Maintain an International Ship Security Certificate (ISSC) - issued by flag state
Port facilities must have a Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP) and Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO)
Port and ship security drills and exercises annually
Cyber risk incorporated into Ship Security Plans per IMO 2017 guidance
regulations.penalties: Detention by Port State Control; denial of entry to port; flag state may invalidate ISSC; criminal penalties under national law

regulations.name: Maritime Labour Convention 2006 (MLC 2006) — ILO Convention C186
regulations.authority: International Labour Organization (ILO)
regulations.summary: The Maritime Labour Convention 2006 (MLC 2006) is an International Labour Organization (ILO) Convention (C186), often called the 'Seafarers' Bill of Rights.' Administered by the ILO — not the IMO — it consolidates 68 previous ILO maritime standards into a single instrument covering seafarers' employment conditions, working hours, accommodation, health protection, medical care, welfare, and social security. Port state control inspections are coordinated with IMO conventions. Entered into force 20 August 2013; ratified by states representing over 96% of gross tonnage.
key_requirements: Seafarer Employment Agreement (SEA) must be provided to every seafarer before joining the ship
Minimum age: 16 years (18 for hazardous work and night work)
Minimum wages per ILO/MLC guidelines (USD 673/month basic rate as of 2024)
Maximum work hours: 14 hours in any 24-hour period and 72 hours in any 7-day period
Minimum rest: 10 hours in any 24-hour period and 77 hours in any 7-day period
Annual paid leave of at least 2.5 calendar days per month of service
Medical care on board and ashore; medical examination certificates
Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC certificate) and Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC) for ships 500 GT+
regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state penalties; ship owner financial liability for medical costs and repatriation

regulations.name: International Safety Management (ISM) Code
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO) - mandatory via SOLAS Chapter IX
regulations.summary: An international standard for the safe management and operation of ships and for pollution prevention. Requires shipping companies to establish a Safety Management System (SMS), appoint a Designated Person Ashore (DPA), and demonstrate compliance through Document of Compliance (DOC) and Safety Management Certificate (SMC).
key_requirements: Implement and maintain a Safety Management System (SMS) covering safety policy, procedures, and emergency preparedness
Appoint a Designated Person Ashore (DPA) ensuring safe ship operations and providing a link between the company and those on board
Define and document responsibilities and authorities under the SMS
Procedures for hazard identification, risk assessment, and accident investigation
Planned Maintenance System (PMS) for hull, machinery, and safety equipment
Emergency response procedures including contingency plans
Document of Compliance (DOC) for the company and Safety Management Certificate (SMC) for each ship, renewed every 5 years
Internal audits and management review of the SMS annually
regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention; flag state may suspend or withdraw DOC/SMC; criminal liability under national law for negligence

regulations.name: International Convention for the Control and Management of Ships' Ballast Water and Sediments
regulations.authority: International Maritime Organization (IMO)
regulations.summary: International convention preventing the spread of harmful aquatic organisms, bacteria, and pathogens transported in ships' ballast water. Ships must implement approved ballast water management measures using either ballast water exchange (D-1 Standard) or a type-approved Ballast Water Treatment System (BWTS).
key_requirements: Develop and implement a Ballast Water Management Plan (BWMP) approved by the flag administration
Maintain a Ballast Water Record Book (BWRB) documenting all ballast water operations
Install an IMO type-approved Ballast Water Treatment System (BWTS) meeting D-2 Performance Standard by the IOPP renewal survey date
D-2 Standard: less than 10 viable organisms per cubic meter (more than 50 microns); less than 10 viable organisms per ml (10-50 microns)
Alternative: ballast water exchange in open ocean at least 200 nautical miles from land and 200m depth (D-1 Standard)
IMO Ballast Water Management Convention (BWM) Certificate issued by flag state
Port State Control may inspect Ballast Water Record Book and sample ballast water
regulations.penalties: Port State Control detention until BWTS installed; fines under national law (U.S. fines up to USD 27,500 per day); flag state penalties

regulations.name: Bank of Israel Directive 357 — Technology and Information Systems Risk Management
regulations.authority: Bank of Israel — Banking Supervision Department
regulations.summary: Bank of Israel Directive 357 on Technology and Information Systems Risk Management establishes binding cybersecurity and IT governance requirements for all Israeli banking corporations. It mandates risk management frameworks covering IT systems, cyber defense, business continuity, and outsourcing of technology services including cloud.
key_requirements: Establish a board-approved technology and cyber risk management policy
Appoint a CISO with direct board reporting line and defined authorities
Conduct annual cyber risk assessment aligned with NIST or equivalent frameworks
Implement multi-layered defense-in-depth controls for critical banking systems
Report material cyber incidents to the Bank of Israel Supervisor within 24 hours
Maintain a tested cyber incident response and business continuity plan
Manage technology outsourcing risk including cloud providers with enhanced due diligence
Employee cybersecurity awareness training and phishing simulation exercises
regulations.penalties: Bank of Israel may impose supervisory measures including directives, fines, and license restrictions on non-compliant banking corporations

regulations.name: Israeli Capital Market, Insurance and Savings Authority Cyber Risk Management Circular
regulations.authority: Capital Market, Insurance and Savings Authority (CMISA)
regulations.summary: The CMISA Cyber Risk Management Circular mandates that all entities regulated by the Israeli Capital Market Authority — insurance companies, pension funds, provident funds, and investment managers — implement a comprehensive cyber risk management program proportionate to their size and systemic importance.
key_requirements: Board-level accountability for cyber risk governance and annual cyber risk reporting
Appoint a CISO or designate a senior responsible officer for cyber
Implement a cyber risk management framework covering identification, protection, detection, response, and recovery (NIST CSF-aligned)
Conduct annual penetration testing and vulnerability assessments
Report significant cyber incidents to CMISA within 12 hours of detection
Maintain cyber insurance coverage proportionate to risk exposure
Establish third-party cyber risk management including InsurTech vendors
Business continuity and disaster recovery planning with annual testing
regulations.penalties: Administrative fines, public censure, and license suspension by CMISA for material non-compliance

regulations.name: Israel Securities Authority Cyber Disclosure and Risk Management Directive for Public Companies
regulations.authority: Israel Securities Authority (ISA)
regulations.summary: The Israel Securities Authority directive requires all companies publicly listed on the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange (TASE) to disclose material cybersecurity risks and incidents in periodic reports, appoint a board-level cyber committee, and establish documented cyber risk management processes.
key_requirements: Disclose material cybersecurity risks in annual and periodic reports to ISA
Immediately report material cybersecurity events affecting company operations or data to ISA
Board of directors must establish a cyber committee or assign cyber oversight to existing committee
Management must conduct annual review of cyber risk posture and report findings to board
Describe the company cyber risk management framework and key controls in annual reports
Disclose third-party cyber incidents where the vendor holds material company data
CISO role recommended; cyber expertise on board or access to external advisors required
regulations.penalties: ISA may impose civil fines, require corrections, or bring enforcement actions for material non-disclosure

regulations.name: Israeli Computer Law (Hok HaMahshevim) 1995
regulations.authority: Israeli Ministry of Justice / Israel Police Lahav 433 Cybercrime Unit
regulations.summary: Israel foundational computer crime law prohibiting unauthorized access to computer systems, data theft, and computer fraud. Establishes criminal liability for hacking, malware distribution, and cybersabotage. Subsequent amendments aligned Israel with the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime.
key_requirements: Prohibition on unauthorized intrusion into computer systems (criminal offence)
Prohibition on disruption, damage, or destruction of computer data or systems
Prohibition on fraudulent use of computers to obtain money, goods, or services
Prohibition on creating, distributing, or possessing malicious software with criminal intent
Organizations must implement reasonable access controls to avoid liability for inadequate protection
Mandatory reporting of unauthorized access affecting critical infrastructure to INCD
Israel is a signatory to the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime — cross-border cooperation
regulations.penalties: Up to 5 years imprisonment for unauthorized access; up to 7 years for computer fraud or sabotage; corporate fines
